title: Helsing raises $1.8 billion at $18 billion valuation in largest European defense-tech round ever
subtitle: The Munich-based drone and autonomy company becomes mainland Europe's most valuable startup, with Goldman, JPMorgan, and CPPIB backing an oversubscribed Series E.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: German defense-AI company Helsing closed a $1.8 billion Series E on July 13 at an $18 billion post-money valuation. New investors include JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs Alternatives; existing backers Lightspeed, Iconiq, Dragoneer, General Catalyst, and Plural returned, with the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and Stepstone also joining. The company said demand significantly exceeded the available allocation.

The size and speed of the round put a floor under European defense-tech valuations. Helsing builds AI-powered targeting software and autonomous systems, including the HX-2 strike drone currently deployed with Ukrainian forces and the underwater surveillance platforms it introduced earlier this year. Its post-money now exceeds every other private company headquartered on the continent — including the general-purpose AI labs Europe has spun up.

The context is a rearming Europe that has decided software-defined warfare is where it can compete. NATO members' collective defense spending crossed 2.7% of GDP for the first time this year, most of that going to procurement rather than personnel. Helsing's pitch to investors — that autonomous drones and AI-assisted command systems change the cost curve of frontline combat — mirrors what Anduril and Palantir have argued in the U.S., but with the political tailwind of a live war on the continent's border.

A note for learners: defense is now one of the fastest-growing consumers of AI talent and compute outside the frontier labs. If you work in ML systems, autonomy, or robotics, the sector is hiring — and the ethical questions are ones you should have thought through before you take the interview, not after.
